# Project Documentation, Reports, Talks, and Tutorials Task Guide

Use this guide for README files, licensing text, contribution guides, final reports, back-cover descriptions, talk materials, tutorials, onboarding materials, or public-facing project summaries.

## Goal

Create polished, professional documents that explain the project clearly to instructors, reviewers, contributors, students, or conference audiences.

## Project framing

The textbook is an open-access, student-centered differential equations textbook built in PreTeXt/Runestone. It emphasizes approachable explanations, short narrative blocks, frequent low-stakes checkpoints, interactive visualizations, optional audio narration, no-cost access, open licensing, instructor adaptability, and modern digital textbook design.

## README and repository docs

A strong README should include:

- project title;
- short description;
- key features;
- repository structure;
- build instructions;
- licensing information;
- contributing guidance;
- acknowledgments;
- contact information or placeholders.

Use clean Markdown. Avoid formatting that breaks after code fences. For build instructions, prefer concise command blocks and explain assumptions.

## Licensing

The project commonly uses CC BY-SA 4.0 for textbook content. When drafting license boilerplate, include attribution placeholders when needed, point to the license text without overloading every file, distinguish textbook content from software code if relevant, and keep chapter-file docstrings concise.

## Reports

For grant or final reports, use a professional but readable tone. Emphasize goals, completed deliverables, accessibility, sustainability, student-centered design, OER value, PreTeXt/Runestone infrastructure, ancillary materials, and future maintenance. Avoid overclaiming evidence when assessment data is limited.

## Talks and presentations

For talks about the textbook or PreTeXt, use a personal, practical, student-centered framing. A useful structure is:

1. why the traditional textbook experience needs rethinking;
2. how PreTeXt provides pedagogical infrastructure;
3. what the differential equations book does differently;
4. examples of student-facing design choices;
5. lessons learned and next steps.

For beginner audiences, keep tooling accessible. Treat GitHub and Codespaces as practical setup tools, not the main intellectual focus.

## PreTeXt tutorials

For beginner PreTeXt tutorials:

- assume no prior PreTeXt knowledge;
- assume some LaTeX familiarity when appropriate;
- use GitHub Codespaces as the fastest setup path;
- keep GitHub workflow minimal and black-boxed;
- guide users toward building and previewing a small lesson;
- include local-install guidance as a later path;
- account for flaky internet or workshop setup issues.

## IRB and research-adjacent documents

When discussing surveys, focus groups, or student data, distinguish instructional activity from research use. Avoid implying data can be used externally without appropriate consent. Mention alternative extra-credit mechanisms when relevant and be precise about time estimates.

## Quality checks

- Audience is clear.
- The document is concise but complete.
- Claims are supported and not overextended.
- Markdown or requested format is clean.
- Project terminology is consistent.
- Readers have a clear next step.
